negative_search_evidence.missing_behavior: No audited repo preserves a canonical scheduler rule that suppresses missed historical windows and fires only the current/next execution index; the port catches up unexecuted due indices inside TTL.
related_but_insufficient_code: The live port uses due_index_bounds and compute_obligations to enumerate all unexecuted due indices inside the TTL window; payload synthesis and block verification consume that multi-index obligation set. No fired_count/current_index exact-target gate was found.

notes: Confirmed gap based on subagent negative search and parent evidence for due-window enumeration.
rationale: The cluster has one candidate and no bounded successor or conflicting candidate. The candidate explains that the canonical scheduler intentionally requires the current execution index to exactly match fired_count, so skipped historical windows remain missed instead of being emitted as a catch-up burst. Because the cited commit is documentation-only, the invariant is current but medium-confidence as lineage evidence.
statement: Canonical scheduling emits a scheduled call only when the current execution index exactly equals fired_count; if fired_count is behind the current index because prior execution windows were missed, those windows remain missed and are not implicitly caught up in a burst.
suggested_falsification_tests: Configure a canonical scheduled call with fired_count lower than the current execution index, run canonical scheduling for the current block, and assert that no transactions are emitted for the skipped indices.

Configure the same call so fired_count exactly equals the current execution index, run canonical scheduling, and assert that only the single due execution is emitted and fired_count advances normally.
summary: Canonical scheduler missed-window suppression is absent; the port enumerates bounded due windows instead.
test_or_falsification_candidate: Create a schedule with missed earlier indices still inside TTL at block 130; current port due_index_bounds can return multiple indices, while the invariant expects no catch-up burst.
title: Missed canonical scheduler windows are not caught up
